old_claim.note: Natural Dyes provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: Pigment Creation provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

source_shape.source_locator: The paper reports a 100,000-year-old ochre-processing workshop at Blombos Cave, predating the 73,000-year-old ochre drawing used to anchor the art node.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Prepared ochre pigment technology is a direct material predecessor for early visual art, while textile dyeing is a later specialized application.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The art node is anchored to ochre mark-making, so pigment creation is the sourced enabling path and natural_dyes should remain scoped to textile dyeing rather than broad visual art.

ontology_before: Art depended directly on natural_dyes, implying textile dyeing was a direct predecessor for early visual expression.
ontology_after: Art depends on early symbolic communication, pigment creation, and stone tool making; textile dyes remain separate from early pigment-based visual expression.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: natural_dyes is no longer a direct prerequisite for art, and pigment_creation is now a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The art node were narrowed to dyed textiles rather than broad symbolic visual expression.
A stronger source showed the earliest art evidence depended specifically on textile dye extraction rather than mineral pigments.
Pigment creation were re-scoped away from ochre and other prepared colorants used in visual marking.
short_reason: Early art is sourced to ochre pigment mark-making, not textile dyes.
